funding_references.funder_name: Special Research Fund from Ghent University

title: Learning from label proportions for a visual matrix speller (ERP)
description: This dataset comprises EEG recordings from 13 healthy subjects performing a visual P300 speller task using a 6×7 character matrix. The study introduces learning from label proportions (LLP), an unsupervised classification approach that eliminates the need for subject-specific calibration data in brain-computer interfaces. Subjects completed three copy-spelling sessions spelling a German pangram sentence, with ERP responses recorded at 1000 Hz from 31 EEG channels. The dataset achieved 84.5% online spelling accuracy without prior calibration, demonstrating the feasibility of calibrationless BCI operation.
methods_description: EEG data were acquired at 1000 Hz sampling rate from 31 channels using a BrainAmp DC amplifier with passive Ag/AgCl electrodes in a standard 10-20 montage (reference: nose, ground: FCz). Subjects performed a visual copy-spelling task using a 6×7 character grid with two stimulus sequences having different target/non-target ratios (3/8 and 2/18). Each character required 68 highlighting events across 4 sequences of length 8 and 2 sequences of length 18. The learning from label proportions (LLP) classifier exploited known class proportions to reconstruct mean target and non-target ERP responses without labeled training data. Trials were extracted from -0.2 to 0.7 s post-stimulus onset (SOA: 250 ms, stimulus duration: 100 ms). Five-fold chronological cross-validation was applied for evaluation.
